title: Stacks & Queues
description: Vec works as a stack (push/pop from the back) and VecDeque as a double-ended queue. These are fundamental for parentheses validation, BFS traversal, and monotonic stack problems in interviews.

sections.title: Vec as a stack (LIFO)
sections.explanation: Vec naturally behaves as a **stack**: `.push()` adds to the top, `.pop()` removes from the top (returns Option<T>), and `.last()` peeks at the top. This LIFO behavior drives parentheses matching, expression evaluation, and monotonic stack problems.

exercise.prompt: Push values 10, 20, 30 onto a Vec stack. Peek at the top with .last(). Pop two elements and print them. Print the remaining stack length.

validation.message: Use push, pop, last, and len for stack operations

exercise.hint: Call .pop() for the second removal. After two pops, only 10 remains.

sections.title: Valid parentheses (classic stack problem)
sections.explanation: The valid parentheses problem: for each opening bracket, push the matching closer. For each closing bracket, check if it matches the top of the stack. This O(n) pattern extends to any bracket-matching or nesting validation problem — a staple of coding interviews.

exercise.prompt: Complete the is_valid function for parentheses checking. Test with "()[]{}", "(]", and "{[]}".

validation.message: "()[]{}" is valid, "(]" is invalid, "{[]}" is valid
Push expected closers, compare pop with current char, check empty at end

exercise.hint: Compare the popped value with the current character c: stack.pop() != Some(c).

sections.title: VecDeque as a queue (FIFO)
sections.explanation: **VecDeque** is Rust's double-ended queue. For FIFO queue behavior: `.push_back()` to enqueue and `.pop_front()` to dequeue. Both are O(1) amortized. This is the standard BFS queue — use it whenever you need first-in-first-out ordering.

exercise.prompt: Create a VecDeque and enqueue 100, 200, 300. Peek at the front. Dequeue two elements and print them. Print the remaining length.

validation.message: Use push_back to enqueue, pop_front to dequeue, front to peek

exercise.hint: Call .pop_front() for the second dequeue. FIFO order: 100 comes out first, then 200.

sections.title: BFS with VecDeque
sections.explanation: BFS uses a queue to explore nodes level by level. Start by pushing the source, then loop: pop from front, process, push unvisited neighbors to back. Track visited nodes with a HashSet to avoid cycles. This pattern solves shortest-path, level-order, and reachability problems.

exercise.prompt: Complete the BFS traversal. Build a graph: 1->[2,3], 2->[4], 3->[4], 4->[]. Start BFS from node 1 and print the visit order.

validation.message: Pop from front, check visited with insert, push unvisited neighbors to back

exercise.hint: Push the neighbor node `next` to the queue: queue.push_back(next).
